questions.title: Multi-Model Confidence Ranking
questions.description: Implement a model ranking function that combines pLDDT, pTM, and ipTM scores to rank multiple structure predictions and select the best model, following AlphaFold3's ranking protocol.
questions.info: AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) generates 5 models using different random seeds and ranks them by confidence metrics to select the best prediction. For monomers, models are ranked by mean pLDDT; for multimers, AlphaFold-Multimer (Evans et al., 2022) uses 0.8 * ipTM + 0.2 * pTM. AlphaFold3 (Abramson et al., Nature 2024) additionally uses a diffusion model confidence score and generates up to 5 diffusion samples per trunk output. Model ranking is crucial because different seeds explore different local minima. The CASP assessment (Kryshtafovych et al., Proteins 2021) showed that model selection can improve median GDT-TS by 2-5 points compared to random selection. Boltz-1 (Wohlwend et al., 2024) uses a similar multi-seed approach with confidence-based ranking for selecting the best prediction from multiple samples.

questions.title: AlphaFold3 Cross-Attention for Conditioning
questions.description: Implement cross-attention from AlphaFold3's diffusion module where noisy atom features attend to conditioning single representations from the trunk, allowing the denoiser to be guided by sequence and evolutionary information.
questions.info: AlphaFold3 (Abramson et al., Nature 2024) uses cross-attention in its diffusion module to condition the denoising process on trunk representations. Noisy atom-level features serve as queries and attend to single-representation keys/values from the Pairformer trunk. This conditioning mechanism ensures the diffusion model generates structures consistent with evolutionary and sequence information captured by the trunk. The cross-attention architecture is similar to the conditioning mechanism in diffusion models for image generation like Stable Diffusion (Rombach et al., CVPR 2022), adapted for 3D molecular structure. AlphaFold3's diffusion module processes atoms at a finer granularity than residues, using an atom-level transformer with cross-attention to residue-level trunk features. This hierarchical approach allows detailed all-atom coordinate prediction while maintaining computational tractability.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

class DiffusionCrossAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_atom=128, d_single=256, num_heads=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = d_atom // num_heads
        self.q_proj = nn.Linear(d_atom, d_atom)
        self.k_proj = nn.Linear(d_single, d_atom)
        self.v_proj = nn.Linear(d_single, d_atom)
        self.out_proj = nn.Linear(d_atom, d_atom)
        self.norm_atom = nn.LayerNorm(d_atom)
        self.norm_cond = nn.LayerNorm(d_single)

    def forward(self, atom_features, conditioning):
        # atom_features: (N_atoms, d_atom) noisy atom representations
        # conditioning: (L_res, d_single) trunk single representation
        N = atom_features.shape[0]
        L = conditioning.shape[0]

        q = self.q_proj(self.norm_atom(atom_features)).view(N, self.num_heads, self.head_dim)
        cond = self.norm_cond(conditioning)
        k = self.k_proj(cond).view(L, self.num_heads, self.head_dim)
        v = self.v_proj(cond).view(L, self.num_heads, self.head_dim)

        # Cross-attention: atoms query, residues are keys/values
        attn = torch.einsum('nhd,lhd->nhl', q, k) / {{prot_struct_new_crossattn_b1}}
        attn = F.softmax(attn, dim=-1)  # (N, H, L)
        out = {{prot_struct_new_crossattn_b2}}
        out = out.reshape(N, -1)
        return atom_features + self.out_proj(out)

atom_feat = torch.randn(500, 128)
cond = torch.randn(100, 256)
model = DiffusionCrossAttention()
print(model(atom_feat, cond).shape)  # (500, 128)

questions.title: Root Mean Square Deviation (RMSD)
questions.description: Implement RMSD computation between predicted and true protein structures after centering. RMSD is the most basic structural comparison metric.
questions.info: Root Mean Square Deviation (RMSD) is the most widely used metric for comparing protein structures, measuring the average distance between corresponding atoms after optimal superposition. RMSD is defined as sqrt(mean(sum_i(d_i^2))), where d_i is the distance between atom i in the two structures. Unlike TM-score (Zhang & Skolnick, Proteins 2004), RMSD is length-dependent, meaning longer proteins tend to have higher RMSD even for accurate predictions. AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) achieves backbone RMSD < 1 Angstrom for many single-domain targets. RMSD is sensitive to outlier residues in flexible regions, which is why metrics like GDT-TS and LDDT were developed. For fair comparison, structures must first be optimally superimposed, typically using the Kabsch algorithm (Kabsch, Acta Crystallographica 1976). All-atom RMSD includes side chains while C-alpha RMSD measures only backbone accuracy.

questions.title: OpenFold Exponential Moving Average of Weights
questions.description: Implement exponential moving average (EMA) of model weights as used in OpenFold's training pipeline. EMA provides more stable model weights for inference by maintaining a running average.
questions.info: OpenFold (Ahdritz et al., 2022) uses Exponential Moving Average (EMA) of model parameters during training to obtain more stable weights for inference. The EMA model maintains shadow parameters that are updated each step as theta_ema = decay * theta_ema + (1 - decay) * theta, typically with decay = 0.999. This technique smooths out training noise and often yields better generalization. AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) similarly uses EMA in its training procedure. EMA is a standard technique in deep learning, popularized by works on semi-supervised learning (Tarvainen & Valpola, NeurIPS 2017) and diffusion models (Song et al., ICLR 2021). For structure prediction, EMA particularly helps stabilize the structure module predictions, which can be sensitive to parameter fluctuations. OpenFold's implementation carefully handles multi-GPU synchronization of EMA weights.

EMA update: theta_ema = decay * theta_ema + (1 - decay) * theta_current.

questions.title: Backbone Dihedral Angle Computation
questions.description: Compute backbone dihedral angles (phi, psi, omega) from four consecutive backbone atom positions using the atan2 formula for torsion angles.
questions.info: Backbone dihedral angles phi, psi, and omega define the protein backbone conformation. Phi is the torsion around the N-CA bond (C_prev-N-CA-C), psi around the CA-C bond (N-CA-C-N_next), and omega around the C-N peptide bond (CA-C-N_next-CA_next, usually ~180 degrees for trans). The dihedral angle between four atoms A-B-C-D is computed using the cross products of consecutive bond vectors: n1 = (B-A) x (C-B) and n2 = (C-B) x (D-C), then the angle is atan2(dot(n1 x n2, normalized(C-B)), dot(n1, n2)). AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) predicts backbone frames rather than dihedral angles directly, but side chain chi angles are predicted explicitly. Dihedral angles are used in Ramachandran validation (Ramachandran et al., JMB 1963) and are the degrees of freedom in fragment-based assembly methods like Rosetta (Simons et al., JMB 1997).

import torch
import math

def compute_dihedral(p0, p1, p2, p3):
    """
    Compute dihedral angle defined by four points.
    p0, p1, p2, p3: (L, 3) or (3,) atom coordinates
    Returns: dihedral angles in radians
    """
    b1 = p1 - p0
    b2 = p2 - p1
    b3 = p3 - p2

    # Normal vectors to planes
    n1 = {{prot_struct_new_dihed_b1}}
    n2 = torch.cross(b2, b3, dim=-1)

    # Normalize b2 for the atan2 formula
    b2_norm = b2 / (b2.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-8)

    m1 = torch.cross(n1, b2_norm, dim=-1)
    x = (n1 * n2).sum(dim=-1)
    y = (m1 * n2).sum(dim=-1)

    return {{prot_struct_new_dihed_b2}}

# Example: compute phi angles from backbone atoms
L = 50
n_pos = torch.randn(L, 3)
ca_pos = n_pos + torch.randn(L, 3) * 0.5
c_pos = ca_pos + torch.randn(L, 3) * 0.5
# Phi: C(i-1) - N(i) - CA(i) - C(i)
phi = compute_dihedral(c_pos[:-1], n_pos[1:], ca_pos[1:], c_pos[1:])
print(f"Phi angles shape: {phi.shape}, range: [{phi.min():.2f}, {phi.max():.2f}] rad")
